paper_title: Microstructure and tensile behavior of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low density steel
citation_sentences: The microstructure of the hot-rolled steel after being solution treated at 700–1000 °C for 1 h is shown in Fig. 1. For H70 (shown in Fig. 1a), nearly all the original austenite phase decomposed to martensite and spheroidal M C -carbide, with a volume fraction of 12.2% being retained. $\mathbf { M } _ { 5 } \mathbf { C } _ { 2 }$ -carbide phase widely distributed both at δ-ferrite grain boundaries and among martensite laths. For H80 (shown in Fig. 1b), $\mathbf { M } _ { 5 } \mathbf { C } _ { 2 }$ -carbide disappeared, martensite has reverse-transformed to austenite. Large amount of strip ferrite, which is termed as α-ferrite in order to distinguish it from δ-ferrite that is formed at high temperature, was observed. For H90, with the solution temperature increasing to ${ 9 0 0 } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , the microstructure of specimen was mainly consisted of α-ferrite, δ-ferrite and austenite, as shown in Fig. 1c. Besides, the morphology of α-ferrite changed from strip shape into fine blocky shape and the volume fraction of austenite increased to 33.8%. For H100, martensite appeared again with only 12.9% austenite retained. The calculated austenite volume fractions for these steels are listed in Table 3. The phases were distinguished according to the Vickers microhardness as is presented in Table 2. And $\mathrm { M } _ { 5 } \mathrm { C } _ { 2 }$ -carbide was identified according to EDS.

paper_abstract: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China
paper_title: Microstructure and tensile behavior of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low density steel
citation_sentences: True stress-strain curves and the corresponding work hardening rate-true strain curves are plotted in Fig. 2. As shown in Fig. 2a, there is no yield point phenomenon occurring under all the solution treatment conditions. With the increase of solution temperature, both the critical stress for uniform deformation and the work hardening rate decreased obviously. However, for H90, true stress increased abruptly at a true strain $\mathbf { o f } \sim 0 . 0 3 ,$ , and both the UTS and TE were the highest among the four steels. As shown in Fig. 3b, the steels possesses an obvious threestage work hardening (WH) characteristics: in stage 1, WH rate decreased rapidly, which was mainly associated with the deformation of ferrite; In stage 2, the slight decrease of the WH rate is a consequence of the strong hardening mechanism, such as TRIP or TWIP effect [24]; In stage 3, the WH rate decreased quickly again, and this was presumably associated with the deformation localization of ferrite or martensite. For H90, the formation of mechanical twining were not observed from SEM analyses. It can be inferred that, possibly, TRIP effect was the main mechanism for high tensile strength and excellent ductility. In stage 2, the WH rate increased with strain in the true strain range 0.03–0.15, because martensitic transformation took place when a critical stress was attained. After true strain of 0.15, the WH rate began to decrease gently, which was the consequence of a potential competition between the increased stress and stress relaxation induced by the TRIP effect.

citation_sentences: True stress-strain curves and the corresponding work hardening rate-true strain curves are plotted in Fig. 2. As shown in Fig. 2a, there is no yield point phenomenon occurring under all the solution treatment conditions. With the increase of solution temperature, both the critical stress for uniform deformation and the work hardening rate decreased obviously. However, for H90, true stress increased abruptly at a true strain $\mathbf { o f } \sim 0 . 0 3 ,$ , and both the UTS and TE were the highest among the four steels. As shown in Fig. 3b, the steels possesses an obvious threestage work hardening (WH) characteristics: in stage 1, WH rate decreased rapidly, which was mainly associated with the deformation of ferrite; In stage 2, the slight decrease of the WH rate is a consequence of the strong hardening mechanism, such as TRIP or TWIP effect [24]; In stage 3, the WH rate decreased quickly again, and this was presumably associated with the deformation localization of ferrite or martensite. For H90, the formation of mechanical twining were not observed from SEM analyses. It can be inferred that, possibly, TRIP effect was the main mechanism for high tensile strength and excellent ductility. In stage 2, the WH rate increased with strain in the true strain range 0.03–0.15, because martensitic transformation took place when a critical stress was attained. After true strain of 0.15, the WH rate began to decrease gently, which was the consequence of a potential competition between the increased stress and stress relaxation induced by the TRIP effect.

In order to observe the TRIP effect, the EBSD analysis was carried out on H90 under different true strains of 0.03, 0.10, 0.20 and, 0.27, respectively. Fig. 3 shows the superimposed maps of phase distribution map and band contrast map, which can be used not only to distinguish ferrite and austenite grains from martensite grains, but also to measure the volume fractions of austenite. Martensite grains usually show a poor band contrast because of their high dislocation density, indicated in black. Austenite was marked in green and ferrite was marked in gray. At various strain stages, the calculated austenite volume fractions were 31.4%, 22.3%, 10.1% and 4.7%, respectively. It can be concluded that deformation induced martensite transformation occurred continuously with the increase of true strain, which was consistent with the above inference. Both blocky and lath-like retained austenite grains were observed at a true strain of 0.03. Blocky retained austenite was mainly located at the triple junction boundaries, as shown in square region, while lath-like retained austenite was always distributed between ferrite laths or embedded in a single ferrite grain, as shown in ellipse region. Along with the deformation, blocky austenite grains disappeared gradually and only a few were retained at a true strain of 0.20. However, at a true strain of 0.27, almost the whole retained austenite was lath-like austenite. In addition, a large portion of small angle grain boundaries (green line) yielding to 51% was observed at this strain rate, while the values of those under other strains (0.03, 0.10 and 0.20 in turn) were 26%, 31%, 45%, respectively. It has been reported that abundant small angle grain boundaries can significantly improve the deformation ability of the material [25].

citation_sentences: Besides, the orientation of retained austenite relative to the loading direction (Schmid factors) was also investigated, and the Schmid factor maps are presented in Fig. 4. The Schmid factor were always used to measure the relative ease with each grain slips. It is well known that grains with the higher Schmid factor will undergo deformation faster because the slip planes in these grains do not need to undergo much rotation before the resolved shear stress reaches a critical value [26]. In this paper, the grains with a high Schmid factor were indicated in red, whereas those with a very low Schmid factor were indicated in blue using rainbow coloring. As shown in Fig. 4a, the relative volume fraction of retained austenite with Schmid factors of 0.45–0.48 was more than 70% at the true strain of 0.03. When the true strain increased to 0.10, the average factor decreased apparently, the majority retained austenite had Schmid factors of 0.39–0.42. With true strain increased to 0.20, over 60% retained austenite had Schmid factors smaller than 0.40, the average Schmid factor of the retained austenite is less than

0.34. However, at the true strain of 0.27, the volume fraction of retained austenite with Schmid factors less than 0.34 decreased to 5%. This means retained austenite possessing relatively small Schmid factors tends to remain untransformed during tensile deformation. This tendency is clearly depicted in Fig. 4e.

citation_sentences: From the perspective of dislocation glide, both the austenite and ferrite were deformed by the dislocation slip at the beginning of deformation. Next, martensite was independently formed inside austenite grain, not only acting as a barrier for dislocation glide but also as a site for dislocation accumulation and as a source of partial dislocations. As the deformation continues, dislocation multiplied constantly in martensite, while wavy glide in ferrite leading to the formation of complicated dislocation structure. This is why high density dislocation were observed in both ferrite and martensite, as shown in Fig. 5b and c. Thus, the dynamic recovery and dislocation climbing were restricted, and the strength performance were enhanced eventually [30–33].

citation_sentences: Two types of austenite grains were carefully analyzed in the present work. Some austenite grains were blocky with a high Schmid factor, and were positioned at the ferrite triple edges. The other ones were lath-like and were located between ferrite laths or embedded in a single ferrite grain with a lower Schmid factor. Fig. 6 shows a schematic illustration of martensitic transformation during tensile deformation for the experimental steel, in which martensite were marked in blue, ferrite delineated in gray, blocky austenite in white and lath-like austenite in red. With the increase of tensile strain, the blocky austenite took the initiative to transform into martensite, which can be attributed to the higher localized stress. It has been accepted that there exist high free energy and atomic activity at grain boundaries and phase boundaries. Consequently, a triaxial state of stress and more strain can be favored on these blocky austenite grains during deformation. In this case, martensitic transformation is prone to take place at ferrite triple junction boundaries [29]. However, for lath-like austenite grains with lower Schmid factor, they would undergo rotations firstly during deformation, which were dependent on the crystallographic orientation of the grains with respect to the straining direction.

citation_sentences: The austenite volume fractions were measured by XRD analysis before and after deformation, as illustrated in Fig. 7. Fig. 7a plotted the XRD profile and the calculated results are plotted in Fig. 7b. In all steels, the austenite volume fraction decreased after tensile tests. As the solution temperature increasing in the range $7 0 0 { - } 9 0 0 \ { ^ { \circ } } \mathrm { C } ,$ the martensite transformation ratio increased gradually, with a peak value of 89.9% in H90. This also suggests an enhanced TRIP effects in association with a metastable stability of austenite.
